Platelet:RBC Ratio by LTOWB Usage 2018-2025

American Red Cross Biomedical Services

Tue 24 March 2026

Temporal Patterns in Platelet‑to‑Red Cell Transfusion Ratios Across Hospitals With Varying LTOWB Utilization

Analysis

LTOWB% by year

Percentage of Hospitals in Each LTWOB Category by Calendar Year - This figure displays the proportion of hospitals falling into each low‑titer O whole blood (LTOWB) utilization category—<2%, 2.5–5.0%, and >5%—from 2018 through 2025. Percentages represent the share of all hospitals reporting data in a given year.

  • Key Findings
    • Growth of higher LTWOB categories over time.
    • Hospitals utilizing LTWOB at >5% or 2.5–5% remained a minority but expanded steadily after 2022.
  • The >5% category increased from 1.4% in 2022 to 18.1% in 2024 and 18.1% in 2025.
  • The 2.5–5% category increased modestly, reaching 11.1% in 2024–2025.

Inflection point in 2022. - In 2022, the <2% category temporarily peaked at 97.1%, coinciding with the lowest representation of higher‑use hospitals (<3% in both mid‑tier and high‑tier categories).
- Beginning in 2023, however, the distribution began to shift as more hospitals incorporated LTWOB at higher percentages.

Emerging adoption pattern after 2023. - Between 2023 and 2025, the proportion of hospitals performing >5% LTWOB usage increased more sharply compared with the 2.5–5% group, suggesting movement directly from low (<2%) to high (>5%) uptake rather than gradual progression.

Trajectory by hospitals
  • Hospital‑Level Trends in Low‑Titer Whole Blood (LTWOB) Use, 2018–2025
Hospital Counts: <2% to >5% LTOWB use

  • Across the study period, several hospitals transitioned from low LTWOB utilization (<2%) to high utilization (>5%).
  • These transitions occurred predominantly after 2023, consistent with the system‑level increase in hospitals adopting higher LTWOB usage in later years

Hospitals without increase in LTOWB use

<2% hospital list
<2% hospital trajectory

Median PLT:RBC by LTWOB%

  • Across eight years of observation, only hospitals with >5% LTWOB usage demonstrated a substantial temporary rise in PLT:RBC ratios, peaking in 2020, with subsequent decline and convergence toward the lower utilization categories by 2023–2025.
  • These descriptive patterns suggest early implementation‑related variability in high‑LTWOB hospitals, with stabilization of transfusion ratios over time.
    • Note: These values represent descriptive medians and do not reflect model‑adjusted estimates or tests of statistical significance.

PLT:RBC by year by LTOWB use

Mixed model: Random intercept for hospital

  • A linear mixed model (hospital random intercept) showed a highly significant LTWOB category × year interaction (p < 1.3e-33),
    • indicating that differences in PLT:RBC ratio across LTWOB adoption levels changed substantially over time.
  • Main effects for year (p < 10⁻⁹) and LTWOB category (p = 0.019) were also significant, but are not interpreted because of the strong interaction.

  • The output shows within‑year comparisons among the 3 LTWOB categories:
    • ‘<2% LTOWB use’
    • ‘2.5–5.0% LTOWB use’
    • ‘>5% LTOWB use’
  • These are LS‑mean (model‑adjusted) differences in PLT:RBC ratio, computed separately for each year.
    • The Tukey method adjusts for the 3 comparisons within each year.
  • 2018: No estimable comparisons.
  • 2019: No differences.
  • 2020: highly significant spike in the >5 category vs both other groups.
    • <2 and 2.5–5.0 do not differ (p ≈ 0.99).
    • Both are dramatically lower than >5 (difference ≈ −3.0 units, p < 0.0001).
    • Conclusion (2020): The hospitals with >5% LTOWB usage have significantly higher PLT:RBC than both other LTWOB groups.
  • 2021: No differences.
  • 2022: Reverse effect: >5 category now has significantly lower PLT:RBC ratio than both 2.5–5.0 and <2. <2 and 2.5–5.0 do not differ significantly. Both have significantly higher PLT:RBC ratio than the >5 category. Conclusion: A reversal compared to 2020. Now hospitals with >5% LTOWB usage have significantly lower PLT:RBC ratio.
  • 2023–2025: No meaningful differences; groups converge in 2023; LTWOB categories remain similar

Model-adjusted means

  • Comparision with the Raw Descriptive Medians (Unadjusted) in Figure 1

  • Figure 1 displays simple medians of the observed PLT:RBC ratio within each LTWOB category and year, without adjusting for:

    • differences in hospital size
    • differences in case mix
    • differences in hospital‑level random effects (baseline transfusion tendency)
    • imbalance in the number of hospitals per category
    • changes in category membership over time

  • Because these medians reflect the observed distribution only, they are susceptible to:

    • influence of a few hospitals with unusually high ratios in that year
    • differences in which hospitals fall into each LTWOB category each year
    • uneven sample sizes across categories
    • regression dilution or skewed distributions

  • Thus, Figure 1 is based on the data as it is, before accounting for clustering and model structure.

  • Figure 2 above is the Model‑Adjusted LS‑means (Mixed Model)

    • displays the least‑squares means from a linear mixed‑effects model with:
      • hospital random intercepts (accounting for repeated measures and baseline differences),
      • a LTWOB category × year interaction,
      • and adjustment for the unbalanced structure of the data.

  • Therefore, the LS‑means represent the expected PLT:RBC ratio for each category and year after controlling for hospital‑level variability.

  • This modeling makes important changes:

    • Removes influence of hospitals with unusually high or low raw values
      • 2022 may contain hospitals with extreme ratios in certain LTWOB categories.
      • Random effects shrink those extremes toward the group mean.
    • Adjusts for differences in the number of hospitals per category
      • If 2022 has fewer hospitals in the >5% category, the LS‑mean is estimated with more shrinkage and may differ from the raw median.

  • Corrects for category switching

    • If some hospitals moved between LTWOB categories from 2020 → 2022, the unadjusted medians reflect this churn, but the mixed model handles repeated measures appropriately.

  • The >5% category shows a markedly lower adjusted mean in 2022, dropping below both <2% and 2.5–5.0%.

  • The 2.5–5.0% group appears elevated relative to others, with wide confidence intervals.

  • In 2022, the mixed model identifies hospital‑level heterogeneity that the raw medians cannot correct for:

    • Hospitals in the >5% category may have negative random effects (lower baselines), pulling their LS‑mean down.
    • Hospitals in the 2.5–5% category may have positive random effects or greater stability, elevating their LS‑mean. +Observed medians in 2022 may be influenced by 1–2 hospitals with extreme values; the model reduces their influence.

Thus, the unadjusted Figure 1 reflects what was observed in 2022, while the adjusted Figure 2 reflects the estimated values after accounting for differences between hospitals, repeated measures, and unbalanced categories.

Effect sizes

Effect sizes as differences vs a reference category by year

“>5 minus <2”
Adjusted Difference (>5% minus 2.5–5%) in PLT:RBC Ratio by Year
Year Contrast Estimate Lower.CL Upper.CL P.value
2018 >5 minus <2 nonEst NA NA NA
2019 >5 minus <2 0.377 -0.330 1.084 0.2955
2020 >5 minus <2 2.991 2.569 3.413 <0.0001
2021 >5 minus <2 -0.187 -0.700 0.327 0.4754
2022 >5 minus <2 -0.927 -1.631 -0.222 0.0101
2023 >5 minus <2 0.009 -0.267 0.284 0.9509
2024 >5 minus <2 -0.066 -0.255 0.122 0.4894
2025 >5 minus <2 -0.046 -0.230 0.138 0.6242
  • These estimates represent adjusted mean differences in PLT:RBC ratio between hospitals with >5% LTWOB usage and those with <2%, generated from a linear mixed model including a hospital‑level random intercept and a LTWOB category × year interaction.
    • Positive values indicate higher PLT:RBC ratios in the >5% group;
    • Negative values indicate lower ratios.
    • Confidence intervals are 95%, with Kenward–Roger degrees of freedom.
    • The adjusted difference in PLT:RBC ratio between hospitals with >5% versus <2%
    • LTWOB utilization varied substantially over time.
  • A pronounced elevation in the >5% group was observed in 2020 (difference, 3.03; p < 0.0001), representing a strong early divergence in component use.
  • By 2022, this pattern reversed, with the >5% group demonstrating significantly lower adjusted ratios (difference, −0.90; p = 0.012).
  • No significant differences were observed in 2019 or 2021, nor in 2023–2025, indicating a return to alignment across LTWOB categories in later years.
  • The adjusted difference in PLT:RBC ratio between hospitals with >5% and <2 LTWOB utilization varied substantially over time (significant interaction). A marked elevation in PLT:RBC ratio was observed among >5% hospitals in 2020, with adjusted differences exceeding 3 units compared with the <2% group (p < 0.0001).
  • By 2022, this pattern reversed, with the >5% group demonstrating significantly lower adjusted ratios (difference ≈ −0.9; p = 0.01).
  • For all subsequent years (2023–2025), the categories showed no meaningful differences, with point estimates clustered near zero.
“>5 minus 2.5-5”
Adjusted Difference (>5% minus 2.5–5%) in PLT:RBC Ratio by Year
Year Contrast Estimate Lower.CL Upper.CL P.value
2018 >5 minus 2.5-5 nonEst NA NA NA
2019 >5 minus 2.5-5 0.386 -0.410 1.183 0.3409
2020 >5 minus 2.5-5 2.980 2.408 3.552 <0.0001
2021 >5 minus 2.5-5 -0.194 -0.827 0.439 0.5475
2022 >5 minus 2.5-5 -1.106 -1.894 -0.317 0.0061
2023 >5 minus 2.5-5 -0.004 -0.363 0.354 0.9814
2024 >5 minus 2.5-5 -0.062 -0.258 0.133 0.5295
2025 >5 minus 2.5-5 -0.042 -0.230 0.146 0.6584
  • The adjusted difference in PLT:RBC ratio between hospitals with >5% and 2.5–5% LTWOB utilization varied substantially over time (significant interaction). A marked elevation in PLT:RBC ratio was observed among >5% hospitals in 2020, with adjusted differences exceeding 3 units compared with the 2.5–5% group (p < 0.0001).
  • A pronounced elevation in the >5% group was observed in 2020 (difference, 3.06; p < 0.0001), representing a strong early divergence in component use.
  • By 2022, this pattern reversed, with the >5% group demonstrating significantly lower adjusted ratios (difference, −1.47; p = 0.003).
  • No significant differences were observed in 2019 or 2021, nor in 2023–2025, indicating a return to alignment across LTWOB categories in later years.
  • For all subsequent years (2023–2025), the categories showed no meaningful differences, with point estimates clustered near zero. > Temporal fluctuations in PLT:RBC ratio among hospitals with higher LTWOB usage suggest early variability in transfusion practices during whole‑blood program rollout, followed by stabilization and convergence across hospitals in subsequent years.
